visible_agent_inputs.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Trout Creek, Utah — [2] quote: "Trout Creek is a small farming unincorporated community"
- Pony Express/Overland route — [2] quote: "Pony Express/Overland route"
- "Letters to Judy: What Kids Wish They Could Tell You" — [6] quote: "Letters to Judy: What Kids Wish They Could Tell You"

Ambiguities:
- "Pony Express/Overland route" bundles two services ("Pony Express" and "Overland") — which delivered what is unclear from the passages.
- The token "Letters" appears as a book title ([6]) and could be conflated with literal mailed "letters".

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do NOT state which items (e.g., "mail", "letters", "packages") were delivered to Trout Creek, nor do they explicitly confirm that Pony Express or Overland services delivered mail/letters to Trout Creek.

Retrieval direction:
- Open the "Pony Express" page (confirm what the Pony Express delivered and its route stops).
- Open the "Overland Mail Company" or "Overland route" page (confirm mail/passenger services and stops).
- Search query: "Trout Creek, Utah Pony Express" (or page "Trout Creek, Utah" for postal history/post office).
- Search query: "Trout Creek Utah post office history" or "Trout Creek postal service" to verify delivered items locally.

Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.

input_dict.current_prompt:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Agent role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

Pipeline position: after summary1 and before second-hop retrieval. Inputs are the original question and the first-hop intermediate state. Functional responsibility: produce one focused retrieval query that is likely to recover the missing evidence needed by the downstream agents. Query quality is determined by evidence recovery, not by whether the query itself resembles or contains the final answer.

Native output contract: The module must consume `question` and `summary_1` and produce exactly one compact second-hop query field named `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include every distinct entity token visible in the inputs in the single terse query and place at most one neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the targeted relation or attribute (for example: "EntityA EntityB director").
- If a visible disambiguator or attribute token appears adjacent to an entity in the inputs (for example a multiword service name, an explicit page title, a year, or an attribute marker like a birth/producer/service/series cue), include that disambiguator token together with the associated entity in the query rather than dropping or truncating it.
- If `summary_1` contains an explicit page-title token sequence (for example an instruction-like ExactPageTitle or Fetch page title), prefer that exact page-title token sequence as the query value.
- When the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.

output.query: Trout Creek Utah Pony Express Overland Letters post office

retrieved_doc_snippets: Trout Creek, Utah | Trout Creek is a small farming unincorporated community, in western Juab County, Utah, United States. It is located along the Pony Express/Overland route in northern Snake Valley, north of Partoun and south of Callao. It is named after the creek that flows from the west off of the Deep Creek Mountains. It has one of the most remote Mormon chapels in Utah, with a short section of paved road, the only paved road for over 50 mi .

Gandy, Utah | Gandy is a small farming unincorporated community in Millard County, Utah, United States, located just east of the Nevada border. It is located in the west-central part of Snake Valley. It is known for Gandy Warm Springs and Gandy Creek, a large spring (15-19 cfs) that comes out of the base of Spring Mountain to the west. It stays around 81–82 degrees Fahrenheit (27–28 Celsius) year-round. Originally known as Smithville, Gandy was renamed in 1925 after Isaac Gandy, the first ranch  ...[truncated]

Hollenberg Pony Express Station | Hollenberg Pony Express Station, also known as Cottonwood Pony Express Station, was built by Gerat H. Hollenberg in 1858, to serve travelers on the Oregon and California Trails. When the Pony Express was established in 1860, the cabin served riders and provided remounts. The station also served the Butterfield Overland Mail. Hollenberg traded with emigrants on the trails, operated the westernmost Pony Express station in Kansas, and provided relay services for th ...[truncated]

Pony Express (TV series) | Pony Express is an American western television series about the adventures of an agent in the 1860s of the Central Overland Express Company, better known as the Pony Express. The half-hour program starring Grant Sullivan was created by California National Productions. "Pony Express" ran for thirty-five episodes in syndication from the fall of 1959 until May 1960.

Mammoth Creek | Mammoth Creek is a Utah creek which flows for over 20 miles through mountains and forests from Mammoth Summit (near Brian Head, Utah), through the Mammoth Valley, to its confluence with the Sevier River (near Hatch, Utah). The creek contains wild brown trout and hatchery rainbow trout.

Tuttle, Colorado | Founded in the late 1800s, Tuttle, Colorado was a US Post Office for the local Pony Express. At its peak, Tuttle housed between 70-80 residents. As the Pony Express fell out of use, the last few residents either moved, or grew old and died. In a 1900 census of Kit Carson County, Tuttle had a population of about 15, including a blacksmith, postmaster, a photographer, and a novelist. The last resident of Tuttle died in 1914. All that remains of this small town are some foundatio ...[truncated]

Pony Express Stables | The Pony Express Stables, also known as Pike's Peak Stables, is a historic stable building located at St. Joseph, Missouri. It is a one story building, originally built as a wood frame structure in 1858. Its exterior walls were rebuilt in brick in 1888, whether keeping the original structure intact and/or reused some of its posts and beams. The building marked the eastern terminus of the Pony Express. It is now the home of the Pony Express Museum.
